foods.name: Chicken

drivers.description: Grows the corn and soy that feed the birds
Powers tractors, feed trucks, slaughterhouse transport
Heats poultry houses and processing plants
Petroleum-derived trays, wrapping, and labels

foods.explanation: Chickens eat ~2kg of grain per 1kg of meat produced. That grain requires fertilizer (30% Hormuz-sourced) and diesel to grow and transport. Every link in the chain amplifies the crisis.

foods.name: Bread

drivers.description: Fertilizer and diesel for large-scale grain cultivation
High energy demand for continuous-run gas ovens
Polyethylene bags are petroleum-derived

foods.explanation: Wheat production is fertilizer-intensive. Industrial bakeries run on natural gas around the clock, directly linking supermarket bread to Hormuz energy flows.

foods.name: Tomatoes

drivers.description: Natural gas maintains growing temperatures in winter
High fertilizer application for intensive horticulture
Diesel-powered cold-chain logistics

foods.explanation: Out-of-season tomatoes depend on heated greenhouses and refrigerated logistics year-round — both tightly coupled to Hormuz energy prices.

foods.name: Salmon

drivers.description: Soy and fishmeal production requires fertilizer and fuel
Air and sea freight for freshness-critical supply chains
Pumps, oxygenation, and cold storage at fish farms
Vacuum trays and modified atmosphere packaging

foods.explanation: Farmed salmon is one of the most energy-intensive proteins. Feed production, cold storage, and long-haul freight combine to create exceptional Hormuz exposure.

foods.name: Rice

drivers.description: Paddy rice is one of the highest urea-consuming crops
Pumped irrigation dominates rice paddy water management
Most global rice trade moves by sea through Hormuz-adjacent routes

foods.explanation: Rice paddies require heavy urea inputs and diesel-powered irrigation. Combined with global shipping disruption, supply is tightening even in traditionally surplus regions.

foods.name: Pasta

drivers.description: High-nitrogen crop requiring significant fertilizer input
Energy-intensive industrial drying and pressing
Bulk grain and finished product haulage

foods.explanation: Durum wheat is a fertilizer-hungry crop, and pasta manufacturing requires substantial thermal energy for drying. Italy's dependence on North African and Eastern European durum adds a Hormuz-adjacent shipping component.

foods.name: Flour

drivers.description: Nitrogen inputs account for ~20% of wheat production cost
Roller mills and conditioning use significant electricity and gas
Grain to mill and flour to bakeries and retailers

foods.explanation: Flour sits at the base of the food chain. Its crisis exposure flows directly through wheat farming costs and the energy used to run mills — two channels where Hormuz prices are highly visible.

foods.name: Oats

drivers.description: Oats need less nitrogen than wheat or corn
Rolling and flaking require moderate energy

foods.explanation: Oats are among the least Hormuz-exposed staple grains. They require less fertilizer than wheat or corn and are largely grown and consumed in northern Europe and North America, limiting maritime shipping exposure.

foods.name: Cornflakes

drivers.description: Maize is one of the most nitrogen-intensive crops
Extrusion, toasting, and coating require sustained gas heat
Lined cardboard boxes and inner bags

foods.explanation: Corn is exceptionally nitrogen-hungry — fertilizer costs represent a significant share of maize production. The industrial cereal manufacturing process adds further energy exposure.

foods.name: Couscous

drivers.description: Origin region (North Africa) relies heavily on Hormuz-linked fertilizer
Couscous manufacturing is a steam-intensive process
Most couscous is produced in North Africa and shipped to Europe

foods.explanation: North African couscous production is squeezed on two sides: fertilizer supply and shipping costs have both surged since February. European consumers see the price impact within weeks.

foods.name: Instant Noodles

drivers.description: Deep-fried noodles use palm oil refined with petrochemical solvents
Asian wheat production is fertilizer-dependent
Manufacturing and frying requires sustained high heat
Multi-layer foil/plastic sachets and cups

foods.explanation: Instant noodles are a Hormuz pressure point hiding in plain sight. Palm oil refining, wheat fertilizer, and multi-layer plastic packaging all draw on Hormuz-disrupted supply chains.

foods.name: Crackers

drivers.description: Nitrogen fertilizer for biscuit-grade wheat
Continuous tunnel ovens run on natural gas
Individual and outer pack wrapping is petroleum-derived

foods.explanation: Crackers are a lower-exposure grain product, but industrial baking energy and packaging costs add meaningful Hormuz exposure on top of fertilizer-linked wheat prices.

foods.name: Beef

drivers.description: Cattle consume 7-8kg of grain per 1kg of beef produced
Machinery, feed transport, and slaughterhouse logistics
Processing, cold storage, and refrigerated display
Vacuum packs, modified atmosphere trays

foods.explanation: Beef has the highest feed conversion ratio of any common protein — 7-8kg of grain per 1kg of meat. Every unit of fertilizer-driven feed price increase is multiplied several times by the time it reaches the supermarket shelf.

foods.name: Pork

drivers.description: Pigs eat ~3kg grain per 1kg of pork — better than beef, still significant
Pig farms, abattoirs, and distribution centres
Heating intensive pig barns, scalding tanks in processing

foods.explanation: Pork's feed conversion is better than beef but still substantial. European industrial pig farms are also large consumers of natural gas for barn climate control, adding another Hormuz channel.

foods.name: Lamb

drivers.description: Improved pasture for intensive lamb production requires nitrogen
Winter and finishing feed is grain-based
New Zealand and Australian lamb shipped globally
Long shipping routes require energy-intensive refrigeration

foods.explanation: Imported lamb (especially from New Zealand and Australia) is doubly exposed: long-haul refrigerated shipping and Hormuz-linked fertilizer for pasture improvement and supplementary feed.

foods.name: Turkey

drivers.description: Similar feed ratio to chicken — corn and soy dependence
Climate control for large turkey sheds
Hatchery-to-farm and farm-to-processor logistics

foods.explanation: Turkey shares most of chicken's supply chain vulnerabilities — intensive grain feeding and gas-heated barns — but with slightly lower turnover rates, meaning feed costs accumulate longer before slaughter.

foods.name: Sausages

drivers.description: Base meat price has risen with grain inputs
Collagen and plastic sausage casings are petroleum-derived
Smoking, cooking, and sterilisation in industrial plants
Diesel cold-chain from factory to shelf

foods.explanation: Processed meat products like sausages stack multiple Hormuz exposures: the underlying meat cost, petroleum-derived casings, gas-intensive cooking, and refrigerated logistics all move together.

foods.name: Milk

drivers.description: Silage and concentrate feed require fertilizer and diesel
Large-scale pasteurisation requires continuous thermal energy
Milk requires unbroken cold-chain from farm to shop

foods.explanation: Dairy farming is energy-intensive at every stage: fertilised silage fields, gas-powered pasteurisation, and a diesel-dependent cold chain. The exposure is moderate because dairy cows are efficient converters compared to beef.

foods.name: Cheese

drivers.description: It takes ~10 litres of milk to make 1kg of hard cheese
Temperature-controlled ageing caves or cellars use constant energy
Wax, vacuum bags, and retail wrapping

foods.explanation: Cheese concentrates milk — and its Hormuz exposure. The 10:1 conversion ratio means every euro of dairy cost increase becomes €10 of cheese cost pressure before processing even begins.

foods.name: Butter

drivers.description: ~20 litres of milk per kg of butter — extreme input concentration
Industrial separators and churns are energy-intensive
Butter requires consistent refrigeration throughout distribution

foods.explanation: Butter requires approximately 20 litres of milk per kilogram. With dairy inputs already squeezed by feed and energy costs, this concentration ratio makes butter one of the most crisis-exposed dairy products.

foods.name: Yoghurt

drivers.description: Milk sourcing drives the majority of yoghurt's input cost
Requires precise temperature control using gas or electricity
Polypropylene pots and foil lids are petroleum-derived

foods.explanation: Yoghurt's Hormuz exposure is driven primarily by milk input costs and polypropylene packaging. Unlike butter and cheese, the lower concentration ratio keeps overall exposure in the moderate band.

foods.name: Eggs

drivers.description: Laying hens eat grain-based feed requiring fertilizer
Temperature control is essential for year-round production
Daily collection rounds to retailers and egg processors

foods.explanation: Eggs share chicken's supply chain vulnerabilities — fertilizer-intensive grain feed and gas-heated facilities — without the option of delay. Production cannot be paused, so cost increases pass through immediately.

foods.name: Cream

drivers.description: Cream is separated from milk — inherits all dairy input costs
Centrifuge separation and UHT treatment are energy-intensive
Tetra Pak-style packaging is petroleum-derived laminate

foods.explanation: Cream concentrates milk's Hormuz exposure through a roughly 3:1 conversion ratio. UHT treatment adds significant thermal energy costs on top of the underlying dairy cost pressures.

foods.name: Potatoes

drivers.description: Potatoes are one of the highest fertilizer-consuming vegetables
Mechanical harvesting is highly fuel-intensive
Long-term cold storage extends availability but adds energy cost

foods.explanation: Potatoes are heavy feeders — per-hectare fertilizer requirements are among the highest of any vegetable. Diesel for mechanical harvesting adds a further layer of Hormuz sensitivity.

foods.name: Onions

drivers.description: Bulb formation requires significant nitrogen application
Planting and harvesting machinery runs on diesel

foods.explanation: Onions are a low-to-moderate exposure crop. Fertilizer and diesel for cultivation are the main Hormuz channels, partially offset by their efficient long-term storage without refrigeration.

foods.name: Bananas

drivers.description: Intensive monoculture requires heavy urea and DAP application
Banana transport requires temperature-controlled container ships
Ethylene-based ripening rooms use energy for climate control

foods.explanation: Bananas travel further than almost any other mass-market fruit. Container shipping disruption and fertilizer costs for tropical monoculture plantations combine to push prices upward.

foods.name: Apples

drivers.description: Moderate nitrogen application for fruit tree orchards
Year-round availability requires controlled atmosphere storage

foods.explanation: Domestic and European apples have limited Hormuz exposure. Orchards use modest fertilizer inputs and are mostly locally distributed. Controlled atmosphere storage adds some gas exposure for out-of-season availability.

foods.name: Oranges

drivers.description: Fertilizer and pest management for citrus groves
Mediterranean citrus production keeps logistics distances short

foods.explanation: Mediterranean-grown oranges have relatively low Hormuz exposure. Short supply chains and moderate fertilizer needs keep this fruit in the low-impact category.

foods.name: Lettuce

drivers.description: Leafy greens require nitrogen but at lower rates than cereals
Short shelf life means local supply chains — minimal shipping

foods.explanation: Lettuce and leafy greens are the least Hormuz-exposed items on this list. Low fertilizer intensity, short supply chains, and minimal processing make them resilient to the current crisis.

foods.name: Bell Peppers

drivers.description: Peppers are grown almost exclusively in heated glasshouses
Intensive horticulture demands high fertilizer inputs
Fragile produce requires unbroken cold chain

foods.explanation: Greenhouse pepper production in northern Europe is almost entirely gas-dependent for heating. At current Dutch gas prices, greenhouse running costs have nearly doubled since February.

foods.name: Carrots

drivers.description: Carrots need modest potassium and phosphate — less Hormuz-exposed
Root vegetable harvesting is mechanised

foods.explanation: Carrots are among the most Hormuz-resilient vegetables. They grow in open fields with relatively modest fertilizer needs, store well without refrigeration, and are mostly grown and consumed regionally.

foods.name: Avocado

drivers.description: Avocados are flown or shipped from Mexico, Peru, and Kenya
High-intensity irrigation and fertilizer for tropical orchards
Ethylene-controlled ripening requires precise temperature management

foods.explanation: Avocados are air-freighted or shipped long distances under refrigeration, making them highly sensitive to fuel price shocks. The shipping distance from Latin America and Africa amplifies the Hormuz effect.

foods.name: Garlic

drivers.description: Nitrogen and potassium for bulb development
Much of EU garlic is imported from China and Spain

foods.explanation: Garlic has low Hormuz exposure relative to most produce. Moderate fertilizer needs and good storage properties limit crisis sensitivity, though import-dependent supply chains carry some shipping cost exposure.

foods.name: Cucumber

drivers.description: Cucumbers are grown in heated glasshouses year-round
Intensive greenhouse cultivation uses high nutrient inputs

foods.explanation: Like peppers and tomatoes, cucumbers are greenhouse crops that depend heavily on natural gas for heating in northern climates, making them moderately exposed to the energy price surge.

foods.name: Shrimp

drivers.description: High-protein aquaculture feed uses fishmeal and soy — fertilizer-intensive
Fresh shrimp is largely air-freighted from Asia, highly fuel-sensitive
Processing facilities in Thailand and Vietnam run on gas
Modified atmosphere bags and IQF plastic trays

foods.explanation: Shrimp is the highest Hormuz-exposed seafood on this list. It combines long-distance air freight from Southeast Asia with energy-intensive processing and fertilizer-heavy aquaculture feed — a perfect storm of crisis exposure.

foods.name: Canned Sardines

drivers.description: Small-scale fishing boats run on diesel
Steel production uses coal and gas — some petrochemical exposure
Sunflower or olive oil in the tin is itself crisis-exposed

foods.explanation: Sardines themselves are wild-caught with modest fuel use and no feed costs. The can, the oil, and processing energy push the Hormuz exposure into the moderate range.

foods.name: Cod

drivers.description: North Sea and Atlantic trawlers consume significant diesel
Filleting plants and blast freezing use gas and electricity
Vacuum-packed fillets and retail trays

foods.explanation: Wild-caught cod avoids the feed-cost burden of aquaculture, but deep-sea trawling is diesel-intensive. Processing, freezing, and refrigerated distribution add gas and packaging exposure.

foods.name: Canned Tuna

drivers.description: Tuna is caught in distant Pacific and Indian Ocean waters — high fuel use
Tin plate steel is energy-intensive to produce
Oil-packed tuna adds a crisis-exposed cooking oil layer
Frozen tuna ships from processing plants in Thailand and Ecuador

foods.explanation: Canned tuna is deceptive. The fish is wild-caught with no feed costs, but the fishing fleets travel tens of thousands of kilometres, and the can itself requires energy-intensive steel and oil filling. Combined exposure is extreme.

foods.name: Cooking Oil

drivers.description: Sunflower, rapeseed, and palm oil are fertilizer-intensive crops
Hexane solvent for oil extraction is a petrochemical product
Deodorising, bleaching, and hydrogenation require sustained heat
PET bottles are made entirely from petrochemicals

foods.explanation: Vegetable oil is crisis-exposed at every step: fertilizer for oilseeds, petrochemical solvents for extraction, gas for refining, and petroleum-derived plastic bottles. It's one of the most Hormuz-sensitive items on any shopping list.

foods.name: Frozen Pizza

drivers.description: All three main components carry significant Hormuz exposure
Tomato sauce and cheese use vegetable oils in processing
Industrial blast freezing and continuous frozen logistics
Film-wrapped cardboard with barrier layers — petroleum-derived

foods.explanation: A frozen pizza is a study in stacked Hormuz exposure. The base (wheat), the cheese (dairy), the meat (feed costs), the tomato sauce (oil), the freezing (gas), and the box (petrochemicals) all move together under the crisis.

foods.name: Chocolate

drivers.description: West African cocoa plantations use fertilizer and diesel
Cocoa beans shipped from West Africa and South America
Chocolate manufacturing requires long-duration thermal processing
Aluminium foil and printed shrink wrap

foods.explanation: Chocolate is a highly traded commodity with long supply chains from West Africa. Cocoa farming costs, transoceanic shipping, and energy-intensive conching and moulding all carry Hormuz exposure.

foods.name: Crisps / Chips

drivers.description: Crisps use high-starch potatoes requiring intensive fertilizer
Frying oil is itself one of the most crisis-exposed inputs
Bags are inflated with nitrogen — produced via energy-intensive separation
Metallised film bags are petroleum-derived and complex to produce

foods.explanation: Crisps are among the most Hormuz-sensitive snack foods. The combination of fertilized potato farming, petroleum-based frying oil, industrial gas usage, and petrochemical packaging stacks exposure to extreme levels.

foods.name: Biscuits / Cookies

drivers.description: Both main ingredients require fertilizer in production
Used as shortening — oil production is crisis-exposed
Continuous gas-fired ovens in industrial bakeries

foods.explanation: Industrial biscuit manufacturing depends on gas-fired tunnel ovens running continuously, palm oil shortenings, and fertilized wheat. Packaging adds a further petrochemical layer.

foods.name: Soft Drinks

drivers.description: Corn-derived sweeteners carry fertilizer costs; cane sugar uses diesel for transport
Polyethylene terephthalate is a direct petrochemical product
Food-grade CO2 is a byproduct of fertilizer production — supply is constrained

foods.explanation: The CO2 used to carbonate soft drinks is a byproduct of ammonia fertilizer plants. When fertilizer production drops due to gas costs, CO2 supply tightens too — a hidden Hormuz linkage.

foods.name: Beer

drivers.description: Barley is a fertilizer-intensive crop; malting uses significant gas
Brewing, pasteurisation, and chilling are gas and electricity heavy
Aluminium smelting is highly energy-intensive

foods.explanation: Beer's exposure stems from the malting and brewing process (gas-intensive) and aluminium can production (one of the most energy-intensive packaging materials). Fertilizer costs for barley add the agricultural layer.

foods.name: Coffee

drivers.description: Coffee is a high-yield crop requiring significant nitrogen and phosphate
Coffee originates in tropical regions far from consuming markets
Coffee roasting is a gas-intensive process
Pressure-sealed bags with degassing valves use complex petrochemical films

foods.explanation: Coffee is exposed at every step: fertilized tropical plantations, long transoceanic shipping, gas-intensive roasting, and speciality vacuum packaging. The high per-kg retail price amplifies the absolute cost impact.

foods.name: Sugar

drivers.description: European sugar beet is heavily nitrogen-fertilized
Sugar refining and crystallisation uses substantial thermal energy

foods.explanation: European sugar beet is a domestic crop but heavily nitrogen-dependent. Refining is energy-intensive. Imported cane sugar adds a shipping dimension. Overall exposure is moderate — well below sweetened products that use sugar as an input.

foods.name: Peanut Butter

drivers.description: Peanuts fix nitrogen but require phosphate and diesel for harvest
Most peanuts are sourced from China, Argentina, or South Africa
Roasting and grinding require sustained gas heat
Glass manufacturing is an energy-intensive process

foods.explanation: Peanut butter stacks long-haul shipping (peanuts are a globally traded commodity), roasting energy, and glass or plastic container production. An underappreciated crisis exposure for a pantry staple.

foods.name: Orange Juice

drivers.description: Brazil supplies ~75% of global OJ — fertilizer and diesel intensive
NFC juice shipped from Brazil requires continuous cold-chain
Tetra Pak-style laminated cartons use petroleum-derived layers

foods.explanation: Not from concentrate orange juice has to be refrigerated all the way from Brazilian orchards to European shelves. That cold chain is diesel-powered. Brazil's fertilizer import dependency (48% Hormuz-linked) adds to the exposure.

foods.name: Mayonnaise

drivers.description: Oil is the dominant ingredient (~70%) — directly crisis-exposed
Egg yolks are the emulsifier — feed costs drive egg prices
Squeeze bottles and jars are petroleum-derived

foods.explanation: Mayonnaise is 65-70% vegetable oil — and cooking oil is one of the most crisis-exposed items on this list. With egg costs also rising, mayonnaise carries compounded Hormuz exposure through both its main ingredients.

foods.name: Tomato Sauce

drivers.description: Processing tomatoes are field-grown with diesel and fertilizer
Industrial tomato processing uses sustained natural gas heat
Both packaging formats are energy-intensive to manufacture

foods.explanation: Field-grown processing tomatoes require less energy than greenhouse varieties, but industrial cooking, packaging, and distribution all add Hormuz exposure. Sauce jars and cans are among the more energy-intensive containers.

foods.name: Baby Formula

drivers.description: Milk solids are the primary ingredient — all dairy Hormuz exposure applies
Spray drying dairy into powder is extremely energy-intensive
Airtight laminated cans with hermetic seals
DHA, ARA, and synthetic vitamins are petrochemical-derived

foods.explanation: Baby formula concentrates dairy's Hormuz exposure and adds spray-drying (one of the most energy-intensive processes in food manufacturing) and petrochemical nutritional additives. The high price-per-kg makes crisis impact particularly visible.

foods.name: Lentils

drivers.description: Lentils fix their own nitrogen — dramatically lower fertilizer need
Combine harvesting and transport to processing

foods.explanation: Legumes like lentils are the most Hormuz-resilient protein source. As nitrogen-fixing crops, they largely bypass the Hormuz fertilizer channel. Short supply chains and long shelf life keep exposure low.

foods.name: Chickpeas

drivers.description: Chickpeas fix nitrogen — very low fertilizer cost
Dried chickpeas ship in bulk without refrigeration

foods.explanation: Chickpeas represent the low end of Hormuz exposure in the food system. Their nitrogen-fixing ability and long shelf life make them a crisis-resilient protein source.

foods.name: Tofu

drivers.description: Soybeans require moderate fertilizer, though less than cereals
Soy soaking, grinding, and coagulation require thermal energy
Water-filled plastic tubs are petroleum-derived

foods.explanation: Tofu is a moderately exposed plant protein. Soy is less fertilizer-intensive than cereals, and the processing footprint is lower than meat — but gas-heated production and plastic packaging add exposure.

foods.name: Dried Beans

drivers.description: Beans fix nitrogen — minimal fertilizer input
Dry beans ship efficiently in bulk without refrigeration

foods.explanation: Dried beans are the most crisis-resilient item on this list. Nitrogen fixation, long shelf life, bulk shipping without refrigeration, and minimal processing make them a genuine Hormuz hedge.

foods.name: Quinoa

drivers.description: Grown at altitude in Peru and Bolivia — moderate fertilizer use
Exported from South America to Europe and North America
Industrial washing and drying uses gas energy

foods.explanation: Quinoa's main Hormuz channel is transoceanic shipping from the Andes. Moderate fertilizer use and processing energy push it into the moderate range — higher than most legumes due to its long supply chain.

foods.name: Olive Oil

drivers.description: Olive trees require less fertilizer than annual crops but inputs are rising
Extraction mills and cooling systems use electricity and gas
Glass manufacturing requires very high kiln temperatures (gas-fired)
Mediterranean production region to global retail

foods.explanation: Olive oil's moderate exposure is largely driven by the energy cost of glass bottle production and distribution. Olive groves themselves are relatively low-input perennial crops — but the packaging tells a different story.

foods.name: Frozen Peas

drivers.description: Peas fix some nitrogen but benefit from phosphate inputs
Industrial blanching uses gas; blast freezing uses significant electricity
Continuous frozen distribution from factory to freezer

foods.explanation: Frozen peas' Hormuz exposure is moderate. Peas partially fix nitrogen, reducing fertilizer sensitivity. But industrial blanching and the frozen cold chain add meaningful gas and diesel exposure.
